top_categories.workout_context: 75 miles on mixed terrain burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
Deccan Cliffhanger's mixed terrain rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Deccan Cliffhanger's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before November.
At Deccan Cliffhanger, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Deccan Cliffhanger. Train it until it feels boring.
Deccan Cliffhanger's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 75 miles.
When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Base fitness for Deccan Cliffhanger. Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.
Varied gradients on mixed terrain demand climbing versatility. Sit the shallow stuff, stand the steep stuff, switch without thinking.
The aerobic base that lets you ride mixed terrain at tempo for 75 miles without cracking. No shortcuts.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what Deccan Cliffhanger's mixed terrain actually does to you.

race_overlay.heat: November in Pune to Mahabaleshwar, Maharashtra, India can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Deccan Cliffhanger. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for Deccan Cliffhanger’s 75 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, TT Threshold, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 75 miles of mixed terrain in Pune to Mahabaleshwar, Maharashtra, India.
